articles.title: Heatwave intensifies across Rajasthan: 47°C warning issued, dust storms and rain likely next week - Bhaskar English
articles.full_text:
Rajasthan braces for season’s harshest heatwave:47°C warning issued, Nautapa begins today
Nautapa, considered the hottest phase of summer, began on Monday with Rajasthan staring at the possibility of temperatures touching 47°C or even higher in the coming days. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued heatwave alerts across several parts of the state, warning that severe conditions are likely to continue for at least four days.
An orange alert has been issued for three districts, while 16 districts remain under a yellow alert on Monday. However, some relief may arrive after May 28, as a western disturbance is likely to become active over northwest India. Its impact could bring cloud cover, thunderstorms, light rain and isolated hailstorms to parts of Rajasthan till the first week of June.
Sri Ganganagar hottest at 46°C, severe heatwave grips western Rajasthan
Rajasthan witnessed intense heat over the last 24 hours, with Sri Ganganagar emerging as the hottest district at 46°C on Sunday. Vanasthali (Tonk) recorded 45.1°C, followed by Alwar at 45°C, Churu at 44.9°C, and Phalodi, Bikaner and Kota at 44.8°C each.
Severe heatwave conditions were reported in Jaisalmer, Phalodi and Bikaner, while Barmer, Jalore, Sri Ganganagar, Hanumangarh, Churu, Jhunjhunu, Alwar, Bharatpur, Dholpur, Karauli, Sawai Madhopur, Kota, Baran, Jhalawar and Chittorgarh also remained in the grip of extreme heat.
Several western districts also witnessed dusty winds blowing at speeds of up to 20 kmph during the afternoon.
Experts predict this week could mark peak summer in Rajasthan
Weather experts believe Rajasthan may witness the peak of summer heat this week before temperatures begin to gradually decline. The activation of a western disturbance in early June, followed by pre-monsoon winds, is expected to increase humidity levels and bring down temperatures, signalling the beginning of humid weather conditions.
Jaipur reels under warm nights, Ajmer temperature may hit 44°C
Jaipur recorded another day of scorching heat on Sunday, with the maximum temperature rising by nearly 1°C to 43.6°C. The city also witnessed a warm night, with the minimum temperature settling at 31.4°C. Heatwave conditions persisted from morning till late evening, and the IMD has forecast similar weather for the next three days.
In Ajmer, both day and night temperatures rose sharply. The maximum temperature touched 41.3°C, while the minimum remained at 30.2°C. The weather department has predicted dry conditions across the division for the next week, with temperatures likely to climb to 44°C.
Udaipur, Jodhpur likely to see further rise in temperatures
Udaipur recorded a maximum temperature of 41°C on Sunday. Although slightly lower than Saturday’s 41.9°C, weather experts said temperatures are likely to rise steadily over the coming week, accompanied by heatwave conditions.
Jodhpur recorded a maximum temperature of 42.2°C, with residents struggling through humid conditions throughout the day. Temperatures may rise by another 2–3°C on Monday.
Kota sprays water on cattle as heat disrupts normal life
Kota remained under severe heatwave conditions, with the maximum temperature touching 44.8°C and the minimum settling at 32.2°C. Intense sunlight and hot winds kept roads relatively deserted during the afternoon.
To protect cattle from the scorching heat, the municipal corporation has arranged regular water sprinkling at cowsheds for nearly two hours every morning and evening. Authorities have also ensured adequate shade and drinking water facilities for animals.
Alwar may touch 46°C, Sikar gets slight relief
Alwar witnessed extreme heat from early morning on Sunday, with temperatures reaching 45°C. Movement in markets and on roads reduced significantly during the afternoon. The IMD has issued a heatwave alert for the district from Monday, warning that temperatures could rise to 46°C in the coming days.
In contrast, Sikar may witness slight relief till May 28. The district recorded a maximum temperature of 43°C on Sunday, while the minimum temperature settled at 22°C. No heatwave alert has been issued for the district so far.
Health risks rise during Nautapa
Doctors have warned that the nine-day Nautapa period significantly increases the risk of heat-related illnesses, affecting everything from the heart to the brain. Experts have advised people to make small but important changes in their diet and daily routine to avoid dehydration, heatstroke and exhaustion.
Health experts have also cautioned that the risk of nosebleeds rises during extreme summer heat due to dry air irritating the nasal passage and rupturing blood vessels.
What is Nautapa?
Nautapa, which began on May 25, will continue till June 2. In Hindu astrology, the period is considered highly significant because the Sun enters the Rohini Nakshatra during this time. It is believed that the Sun’s rays fall most directly on Earth during these nine days, leading to extreme heat and intense heatwave conditions.
According to traditional belief, Nautapa also gives indications about the upcoming monsoon and rainfall season.
